title: Properties of Matter, Fluids and Thermal Expansion
subject: Physics
content:
# Properties of Matter, Fluids and Thermal Expansion

## Quick Synopsis

This area covers elasticity, fluid pressure, buoyancy, viscosity, surface tension, and expansion. Questions are usually direct but require careful units and assumptions.

## Must Know

- Stress is restoring force per area; strain is fractional deformation.
- Young, bulk, and shear moduli measure resistance to length, volume, and shape changes.
- Pressure in a static fluid increases with depth.
- Buoyant force equals weight of displaced fluid.
- Viscosity causes drag in fluids; terminal velocity occurs when net force becomes zero.
- Surface tension acts like force per unit length and causes excess pressure across curved surfaces.

## Formulas / Rules

- Young modulus Y = stress/strain = (F/A)/(ΔL/L).
- Fluid pressure: P = P0 + ρgh.
- Buoyant force: FB = ρfluid Vdisplaced g.
- Stoke drag: F = 6πηrv; terminal velocity for small sphere: vt = 2r²(ρ - σ)g / 9η.
- Excess pressure: drop = 2T/R; soap bubble = 4T/R.
- Linear expansion: ΔL = αLΔT.

## JEE Focus

- Use Pascal law for hydraulic lift questions.
- Use Bernoulli only for steady, incompressible, non-viscous streamline flow.
- In capillarity, decide rise/fall from contact angle.
- For composite rods, enforce equal stress/strain constraints depending on arrangement.

## Common Traps

- Pressure is scalar even though force is vector.
- Upthrust depends on displaced fluid, not object density directly.
- Bernoulli is not valid across turbulent or highly viscous regions.
- A soap bubble has two surfaces; a liquid drop has one.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you solve floating body fraction submerged?
- Can you apply Bernoulli with continuity?
- Can you calculate elastic elongation?
- Can you distinguish drop and bubble excess pressure?
